I was on HMI monitoring the cell when I noticed the part looked a bit discolored and the rotational speed was reading 1285.0 rpm, which is higher than usual. The torque was reading 62.6 Nm, that's pretty close to the max. The line just shut down unexpectedly and I called maintenance to come and take a look.

Likely cause: Overstrain: torque×wear 11456 minNm exceeds the 11000 minNm limit for variant L. Immediate action: Halt the job and replace the tool before mechanical damage propagates."}

I noticed a loud grinding noise coming from the cell HMI during my final walk-through, and the product on the line looked a bit misshapen. The rotational speed was reading 1399.0 rpm and the torque was reading 58.0 Nm, which I thought was a bit off. The tool had been running for 190 minutes, and I decided to swap it out as a precaution since I'm on the day shift and the next shift needs to know about it.

I stopped line 3 at 14:45 because the L51791 tool on the last station suddenly started making this loud screeching noise, and the torque was reading 56.2 Nm. I also noticed that the tool was vibrating pretty bad, and it looked like it was really digging in, with tool wear at 146 minutes.

I was running the M19580 line when I noticed the product coming off the cell had a slight discoloration, and the sound of the tool was a bit different than usual. The temperature difference between air and process was reading 8.3 K, and torque was reading 44.1 Nm. I stopped the line and called maintenance to take a closer look. It's been a long day, and the sun is setting outside, but I wanted to make sure everything is okay for the next shift.

I called maintenance at 14:45 when the process temperature spiked to 312.2 K and torque hit 9.7 Nm. Tool looked fine, but we'd been running at 143 minutes and the machine was vibrating. The air temperature was 301.0 K, so I didn't think it was a heat issue. I stopped production and paged my supervisor, John, as we were nearing the end of the shift anyway.

The line started acting up just after shift change, and I was getting ready to leave when I noticed the product coming off the machine looked a little off - there was some excessive wear on the part. I went to check the HMI and saw that the rotational speed was at 1284 rpm, which is pretty close to spec, but the torque was reading 70.6 Nm, and that's a bit higher than usual. The air temperature was 303.5 K, and the process temperature was 312.4 K, so that's within a reasonable delta T. When I looked closer, I could see that the tool was looking pretty worn, it's been going for 94 minutes now. I stopped the line to take a closer look and called maintenance to come take a look.

I took over at 0600, and the line was running steadily. At 0650, I noticed a slight increase in torque, and it was reading 33.1 Nm, which seemed a bit higher than usual. As I looked at the product coming off the cell, I saw that it looked fine, but the sound of the machine was a bit different, more of a whine than the normal hum. By 0700, the rotational speed was down to 1553 rpm, and the tool was making a loud scraping noise, which was when I stopped the line and paged maintenance. We're getting a new shift in, and I'm heading out.
